text:
China Holds The Key

1⃣In April, China’s independent refineries were instructed to run harder whilst the state-owned entities sold crude in the market and tapped on SPR

2⃣China’s onshore crude inventories were heard to be essentially flat even though crude oil imports were lower. Saudi allocations for June loaders is at only 12-14 mb compared to 2025 average of 45 mb 

3⃣With US pressuring Iran more with sanctions on China’s independent refineries (notably on Hengli which is a 400 kbd integrated petchem-refinery) and the teapots running at negative margins, it is hard to see how this trend can be sustained

4⃣China would need to come back to the market to procure crude, and we expect crude FOB diffs to increase in May trading 

#oott

This failure would immediately translate into an American decision to execute a massive aerial operation (bombing 25% of the remaining Iranian targets). text: Our US crude oil production model shows production growth to ~14.1 million b/d by Q4 2026. This is an increase of ~0.3 million b/d from exit Q4 2025.

I think there's one line item in the global oil supply & demand model that no one is talking about: processing gains.

These are the gains and losses in refining and marine transportation.

This is usually a supply addition line item. This is not a small figure as it's 2.4 million b/d.

With refinery run cuts, the impact to product storage is actually worse than what's on the surface because of the reduction in processing gains. What we are seeing right now is a historic drawdown in product storage, and refineries have to increase throughput to increase processing gains.

For those people who are saying demand destruction is near 5 million b/d, you have to assume a similar proportional decrease in processing gains when assessing the total oil balance.

Our real-time data on demand suggests demand loss is not anywhere near ~5 million b/d. You just have to look at mobility data to see that.

It is what it is.
Uh oh, crude is tightening again.
There it is.

#Brazil: Employment in the Brazilian oil industry has rebounded to the highest since the golden era of Petrobras’s mega-projects in 2010.

The buildout of offshore discoveries including Buzios has boosted investment and created a talent shortage in Latin America’s biggest oil-producing country, said Telmo Ghiorzi, head of trade group ABESPetro. Tens of billions of dollars in spending has allowed the sector to overcome setbacks from a decade ago that included a massive corruption scandal, an oil-price crash, and a prolonged halt in exploration licenses, he said.

Employment levels are expected to stabilize for a few years before beginning to decline in the early 2030s after state-controlled Petroleo Brasileiro SA finishes current projects in the deepwater region known as the pre-salt
#oott https://t.co/W3Sgvf0vvF

Nearly half of the crude oil released from the US Strategic Petroleum Reserve is being exported, a fresh sign of how severely global supplies have tightened amid the Iran war.

About 13 million barrels from the US emergency stockpile have sailed to Europe and other destinations, according to data from Kpler Ltd. based on US Customs’ documents. That represents roughly 40% of the crude released so far from the reserve.

The last tanker to load crude from the SPR was the Kyrakatingo, which took 700,000 barrels of Bryan Mound Sour oil, a variety named after one of the four underground salt caverns where US emergency supplies are stored.

The physic market is showing signs of a slight recovery. 

North Sea window diffs have continued their gradual rebound this week, and today, Chevron kicked off bids across the board. 

CPC loadings for June are also expected to drop by more than 15% compared to May. But it’s still not enough! 

Whether the catalyst is disappointment from the Beijing talks or just pure physics, we need to clear the overhang of the 14 remaining Angolan June cargoes first. 

There are processes in place will force the mkt to play catch-up. I’m still waiting patiently.

A peculiar tanker movement has been observed. The VLCC Huge, owned by the National Iranian Tanker Company(NITC), has resurfaced after 10 days. 

This tanker loaded 2mb of crude at Kharg on April 1 and managed to exit the SoH just before the US blockade took effect.

After last being spotted passing through the Lombok Strait on May 3, it vanished for 10 days before reappearing in the South China Sea. 

What’s striking is its choice of the Lombok Strait over the traditional Malacca route, likely an attempt to evade enforcement risks in Malacca. 

The ingenuity of the Iranians is becoming increasingly resourceful, though it won't be enough to resolve all the difficulties they face.

Iran remained one of the largest producers in OPEC in April

🔹️ The latest monthly OPEC report showed that in April, Iran, along with Saudi Arabia, was among the largest oil producers in the group. https://t.co/DnbO4tlhre

Judy, OPEC influences oil prices. It does not magically set the number on the gas pump like a restaurant menu.

Gas prices are shaped by crude oil, refining capacity, shipping, taxes, inventories, demand, geopolitical risk, tariffs, and market speculation.

So yes, OPEC matters.

But pretending OPEC alone controls what Americans pay at the pump is economics with training wheels.

@EricLDaugh Energy security and nuclear proliferation are serious issues, but linking oil prices directly to hypothetical scenarios is speculative. Markets respond to many factors at once—supply, demand, OPEC policy, and global stability.
